elements.sent-2411-001: Despite the success of text retrieval in many NLP tasks, code retrieval remains a largely underexplored area.
elements.sent-2411-002: Most text retrieval systems are tailored for natural language queries, often neglecting the specific challenges of retrieving code.
elements.sent-2411-003: This gap leaves existing models unable to effectively capture the diversity of programming languages and tasks across different domains, highlighting the need for more focused research in code retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this, we introduce \modelname, a family of large-scale code embedding models ranging from 400M to 7B parameters.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our novel training pipeline unifies multiple programming languages and transforms various code-related tasks into a common retrieval framework, enhancing model generalizability and retrieval performance.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our 7B model sets a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) in code retrieval, outperforming the previous leading model, Voyage-Code, by over 20$\%$ on CoIR benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition to excelling in code retrieval, our models demonstrate competitive performance on the widely adopted BeIR text retrieval benchmark, offering versatility across domains.
elements.sent-2411-008: Experimental results demonstrate that improving retrieval performance significantly enhances end-to-end Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) performance for code-related tasks.
elements.sent-2411-009: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional performance across numerous Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks.
elements.sent-2411-010: However, they often struggle to produce faithful answers and may lack up-to-date or domain-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-011: To bridge this gap, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)~\cite{cai2022recent,cheng2024lift techniques have gained prominence, integrating Information Retrieval (IR) systems with LLMs to enhance their access to relevant external information.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This synergy has drawn significant attention recently, leading to the development of various retrieval models~\cite{wang2022text,chen2024m3 based on BERT~\cite{kenton2019bert and other LLMs with sizes exceeding 1 billion parameters~\cite{wang2023improving,moreira2024nv,SFRAIResearch2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Despite these advancements, standard IR methods, while effective in text-based retrieval, often fall short in specialized domains such as code retrieval~\cite{husain2019codesearchnet .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Code retrieval is critical for accelerating development processes and improving code quality.
elements.sent-2411-015: Unlike general text retrieval, code retrieval enables developers to quickly locate relevant code snippets, explanations, bug analyses, summaries, and similar instances.
elements.sent-2411-016: Effective code retrieval systems are now integrated into commercial products like VS Code~\cite{del2021introducing and GitHub Copilot~\cite{wermelinger2023using,yeticstiren2023evaluating , enhancing productivity.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Code-RAG systems~\cite{parvez2021retrieval,liuretrieval,jimenezswe,wang2024coderag also leverage code retrieval to minimize hallucinations in generated code from LLMs, ensuring more accurate outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, traditional text retrieval models often struggle with code because they focus on linguistic patterns, while code retrieval must handle elements like syntax, variable dependencies, control flow, and API usage.
elements.sent-2411-019: Despite the importance of code-specific models, existing ones like CodeBERT~\cite{feng2020codebert , CodeGPT~\cite{lu1codexglue , and UniXcoder~\cite{guo2022unixcoder are based on smaller BERT models~\cite{kenton2019bert .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: While large-scale models for retrieval have become popular, only Voyage-Code has followed this approach for code retrieval, but it remains a closed model, leaving a gap for open-source, large-scale code retrieval models.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this work, we introduce \modelname, a family of open-source embedding models tailored for both code and text, available in sizes of 400 million, 2 billion, and 7 billion parameters.
elements.sent-2411-022: Our approach handles 12 programming languages and five distinct code retrieval categories across eight different code tasks, including code-to-text, text-to-code, code-to-code, text-to-text and hybrid text and code tasks.
elements.sent-2411-023: This comprehensive setup enables \modelname to generalize effectively across various code domains.
elements.sent-2411-024: Our contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-025: We propose a generalizable training approach for code embedding that transforms diverse programming languages and code-related tasks into retrieval tasks.
elements.sent-2411-026: This method allows for a unified framework that effectively handles multiple languages and task types, improving retrieval performance across various code tasks.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our 7B model, the open-source code embedding model to date, achieves state-of-the-art performance on code retrieval CoIR benchmark, setting a new standard for code retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-028: We also evaluate \modelname on RepoEval and SWE-Bench-Lite, demonstrating that improved retrieval significantly enhances end-to-end Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) performance for code tasks.
elements.sent-2411-029: In addition to the 7B model, we train smaller models (400M and 2B) that outperform the previous state-of-the-art in code retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-030: Furthermore, our models demonstrate competitive performance on text retrieval, making them versatile across both code and text retrieval tasks.
elements.sent-2411-031: We transform general code-related tasks into a unified retrieval framework by categorizing them into distinct retrieval settings.
elements.sent-2411-032: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:frame , each setting serves a specific purpose in mapping between code and text, enhancing the model’s ability to generalize across various retrieval tasks.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{figure/code-embed.pdf} \caption{The code training data of \modelname contains four parts: Text-to-Code, Code-to-Code, Code-to-Text and Hybrid Code. Each Categories contains several types of code tasks.} \label{fig:frame}
elements.sent-2411-033: The Text-to-Code retrieval setting focuses on retrieving relevant code documents based on a given textual query.
elements.sent-2411-034: This setup is particularly useful in scenarios where natural language descriptions are mapped to code solutions.
elements.sent-2411-035: Several code generation tasks can be transformed to fit into this retrieval paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-036: One such task is code contest generation~\cite{billah2024large,kadir2024exploring , where a natural language description of a programming problem is provided, and the goal is to retrieve or generate a code snippet that correctly solves the problem.}
elements.sent-2411-037: This setting closely aligns with the Text-to-Code retrieval framework as it involves finding code solutions based on a question or problem statement.
elements.sent-2411-038: Another example is the Text-to-SQL task~\cite{finegan2018improving,li2024can , where the aim is to generate or retrieve a SQL query based on a user's natural language question.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The task fits well within the Text-to-Code category, as it requires understanding the intent of the query and retrieving or generating the appropriate SQL code that matches the given description.. Code-to-Text Retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-040: The Code-to-Text retrieval setting is designed to retrieve relevant textual documents or descriptions based on a given code query.
elements.sent-2411-041: This setup is particularly valuable in scenarios where developers or systems seek to understand or explain code by mapping it to human-readable documentation or summaries.
elements.sent-2411-042: One such task is code summarization~\cite{sontakke2022code,sun2024extractive , where a code file, or even an entire repository, is provided as input, and the goal is to generate a concise textual summary of the code's functionality.}
elements.sent-2411-043: This task aligns with the Code-to-Text retrieval setting, as it involves taking potentially complex code and producing a human-readable description or explanation, which is critical for documentation, knowledge sharing, and onboarding in software projects.. Code-to-Code Retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-044: The Code-to-Code retrieval setting focuses on retrieving relevant code documents based on a code query.
elements.sent-2411-045: This retrieval setting is particularly useful for tasks that involve understanding, transforming, or complementing code snippets.
elements.sent-2411-046: Several tasks naturally align with this framework, making it an essential part of the broader code retrieval ecosystem.
elements.sent-2411-047: One such task is code translation~\cite{pan2024lost,karanjai2024solmover , where the goal is to translate code snippets from one programming language to another.}
elements.sent-2411-048: For instance, a developer might provide a code snippet in Python and seek to translate it into C++ or Java.
elements.sent-2411-049: This task fits well into the Code-to-Code retrieval setting, as it involves finding the equivalent functionality in a different programming language, ensuring the translated code maintains the same logic and structure.
elements.sent-2411-050: Another important task is code completion~\cite{ding2024crosscodeeval,phan2024repohyper,liurepobench , where a partially written code snippet is provided, and the system needs to retrieve or generate the missing portions to complete the function or module.}
elements.sent-2411-051: This is commonly seen in integrated development environments (IDEs) where autocomplete features assist developers by suggesting the next logical lines of code based on the current context.
elements.sent-2411-052: Code completion aligns perfectly with the Code-to-Code retrieval setting, as it requires matching the partial input code with relevant snippets that seamlessly complement the existing code.
elements.sent-2411-053: Additionally, code clone detection~\cite{martinez2024source , which involves identifying code snippets that are functionally or structurally identical but may differ in syntax or formatting, also fits within this retrieval framework.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Clone detection is crucial for identifying redundant code, refactoring codebases, and ensuring consistency across different sections of a project.
elements.sent-2411-055: This task leverages the Code-to-Code retrieval setting by retrieving code snippets that exhibit similar or identical behavior, even if they appear different at the surface level.. Hybrid Code Retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-056: The hybrid code retrieval setting involves retrieving a combination of both code and textual documents in response to a hybrid query, which may contain both code and natural language.
elements.sent-2411-057: This setting is particularly useful for tasks that require understanding and integrating both code and textual context to generate meaningful responses or retrieve relevant documents.
elements.sent-2411-058: One such task is code agent conversation~\cite{arteaga2024support,jin2024teach , where a code agent must generate either a code snippet or a textual response based on a user's question.}
elements.sent-2411-059: This task requires the agent to interpret the user's question, which might contain both code and natural language, and retrieve or generate the appropriate code solution or explanation.
elements.sent-2411-060: The hybrid query allows the agent to navigate between technical (code) and descriptive (textual) domains to provide a comprehensive response.
elements.sent-2411-061: Another key task is code issue fixing~\cite{yang2024swe,jimenezswe , where a hybrid query containing a description of the code issue and the problematic code snippet is provided.}
elements.sent-2411-062: The goal is to generate code that resolves the issue.
elements.sent-2411-063: By incorporating both the textual description (e.g., error message) and the code itself, hybrid retrieval allows the model to identify relevant code snippets, detect similar issues, and propose fixes that align with the overall project.
elements.sent-2411-064: This makes hybrid retrieval essential in complex development environments, where both debugging and problem-solving require a nuanced understanding of both code and text.. Text-to-Text Retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-065: In order to capture the model's general retrieval ability across both code and natural language domains, we incorporate text-to-text retrieval data into the training pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-066: This allows the model to perform well in traditional text retrieval tasks, enhancing its versatility.
elements.sent-2411-067: The experiments study the ratio of text and code data to determine the optimal balance between the two, aiming to ensure the model's effectiveness in both text and code retrieval tasks without sacrificing performance in either domain.. Retrieval Training.
elements.sent-2411-068: To train a unified code and text retrieval model, we transform all the aforementioned tasks into a query-and-answer format, where either the query or the answer can be a text or code document.
elements.sent-2411-069: This unified format allows us to handle a variety of tasks such as Text-to-Code, Code-to-Text, and Code-to-Code retrieval within the same training framework.
elements.sent-2411-070: The training loss is designed as a contrastive loss~\cite{liu2021dense , which aims to maximize the similarity between the query and the correct answer while minimizing the similarity to the negative samples.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The loss function is represented as: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L} = - \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Bigg[ \log \frac{\exp(q_i \cdot d_i^+)} {\exp(q_i \cdot d_i^+) + \sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp(q_i \cdot d_j^-)} \Bigg] \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \( q_i \) represents the query, which can be either text or code.
elements.sent-2411-072: The term \( d_i^+ \) refers to the corresponding positive document, which could also be in the form of text or code.
elements.sent-2411-073: On the other hand, \( d_j^- \) denotes a set of hard negative documents, which are selected to be similar in context to the query but incorrect, making the retrieval task more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-074: The similarity between the query and a document is measured by \( \text{sim (q, d) \).}
elements.sent-2411-075: The variable \( N \) represents the total number of training samples, indicating the number of queries used during training, while \( K \) defines the number of hard negatives associated with each query.
elements.sent-2411-076: We mainly use two benchmarks to evaluate code and text retrieval performance.
elements.sent-2411-077: COIR~\cite{li2024coir is a comprehensive benchmark specifically designed for code retrieval tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-078: COIR covers a wide range of retrieval challenges, including 8 fine-grained retrieval subtasks, spanning 14 major programming languages.
elements.sent-2411-079: The dataset is composed of 10 distinct datasets, with a total corpus exceeding 2 million entries.
elements.sent-2411-080: BEIR~\cite{thakur2beir is a widely-adopted benchmark designed for text retrieval tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-081: BEIR encompasses a diverse set of retrieval challenges, covering 9 distinct tasks across various domains such as question answering, duplicate detection, fact-checking, and more.
elements.sent-2411-082: It supports retrieval over a wide range of datasets and provides a standardized benchmark for evaluating text retrieval models across different domains.. Implementation Details.
elements.sent-2411-083: We conduct general training on our proposed code and text pair dataset using three model sizes: 400M, 2B, and 7B.
elements.sent-2411-084: For the \smodel, we use the base model Alibaba-NLP/gte-large-en-v1.5~\citep{li2023towards , applying full model fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-085: For the \mmodel, we initialize our embedding model from the generation model google/gemma-2-2b-it~\cite{team2024gemma , using low-rank adaption(LoRA)~\cite{hulora with a rank of 8.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: For the \lmodel, we initialize our embedding model from the generation model mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, also using LoRA with a rank of 8.
elements.sent-2411-087: Following prior work~\cite{SFRAIResearch2024 , we apply: (i) last token pooling for \mmodel and \lmodel, and (ii) beginning token pooling for \smodel to generate semantic vector representations~\cite{li2023towards .}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Cosine similarity is used to compute the similarity between query and corpus for ranking.
elements.sent-2411-089: The batch size is set to 1024 across all three model sizes, with 7 hard negatives.
elements.sent-2411-090: The learning rate is set to $5e^{-5}$, and the end learning rate to $5e^{-6}$, with linear decay and a 50-step warmup.
elements.sent-2411-091: To improve training efficiency and reduce GPU memory usage, we adopt gradient caching~\cite{gao2021scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-092: The more implementation details can be found in Appendix~\ref{section:implementation .. Baseline Models.}
elements.sent-2411-093: For code-domain-specific models, we included UniXcoder~\citep{guo2022unixcoder and Voyage-Code-002\footnote{\url{https://blog.voyageai.com/2024/01/23/voyage-code-2-elevate-your-code-retrieval/ , both of which are pre-trained on code data, making them strong baselines for comparison.}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: For general retrieval models, we evaluated E5-Base~\citep{wang2022text , GTE-Base~\citep{li2023towards , BGE-Base~\citep{xiao2023c , Contriever~\citep{izacardunsupervised , E5-Mistral~\citep{wang2023improving , BGE-M3~\citep{chen2024m3 , NV-Embed-V2~\cite{moreira2024nv , SFR-V2~\cite{SFR-embedding-2 and OpenAI-Ada-002\footnote{\url{https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/embeddings .. Evaluation Metrics.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: In code retrieval, selecting the right evaluation metric is key for assessing both ranking sensitivity and relevance.
elements.sent-2411-096: Building on prior work~\cite{wang2013theoretical , Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) is preferred for its ability to account for both rank order and varying relevance, unlike precision, recall or MRR.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Therefore, we use NDCG@10 to evaluate performance on CoIR and BEIR.

elements.sent-2411-118: In this section, we explore how different retrievers influence the final code completion and issue resolution performance in repository-level tasks.
elements.sent-2411-119: To address this, we utilize RepoEval~\citep{zhang2023repocoder for repository-level code completion.}
elements.sent-2411-120: While RepoEval consists of three splits (function, class, and module), we report results only on the function split, as it is the only one that supports execution-based evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-121: We adopt Pass@1 as our evaluation metric, which measures the accuracy of the top-1 generated code passing the provided test cases.
elements.sent-2411-122: For code generation, we supply the top-5 retrieved code snippets as input to the GPT-3.5-turbo generation model.
elements.sent-2411-123: As shown in Table~\ref{repoeval , all sizes of \modelname outperform the canonical setup.}
elements.sent-2411-124: While some files may not contain direct solutions, as in the canonical documents, they often include valuable function definitions or usage examples that improve code generation outcomes.

elements.sent-2411-178: Despite the success of text retrieval in many NLP tasks, code retrieval has received comparatively less attention than text retrieval.

elements.sent-2411-181: To address this gap, we introduce \modelname, a family of large-scale code embedding models ranging from 400M to 7B parameters.

elements.sent-2411-185: Furthermore, our findings highlight that advancements in retrieval quality directly improve end-to-end Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) performance for code-related tasks.
elements.sent-2411-186: To bridge this gap, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)~\cite{cai2022recent,cheng2024lift,nguyen2024sfr techniques have gained prominence, integrating Information Retrieval (IR) systems with LLMs to enhance their access to relevant external information.}

elements.sent-2411-191: However, traditional text retrieval models struggle with code-specific elements.

elements.sent-2411-193: Furthermore, a unified model capable of handling both text and code retrieval is essential for seamless integration, enabling developers to retrieve documentation, explanations, and relevant code within a single framework.
elements.sent-2411-194: In this work, we introduce \modelname\footnote{Model weights: \url{https://huggingface.co/Salesforce/SFR-Embedding-Code-2B_R , \url{https://huggingface.co/Salesforce/SFR-Embedding-Code-400M_R .}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: To enable the model to retrieve both text and code effectively, we propose a novel multi-stage training method.
elements.sent-2411-196: In the first stage, we train the model on text data using LoRA adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-197: In the second stage, we continue training with both code and text data while tuning only the newly introduced LoRA adapter.
elements.sent-2411-198: This approach preserves the text retrieval knowledge learned in the first stage while enhancing code retrieval, ensuring the model retains strong performance across both domains.
elements.sent-2411-199: We introduce a generalizable multi-stage training approach for code and text embedding, unifying diverse code-related tasks within a retrieval framework.
elements.sent-2411-200: This leads to significant improvements in retrieval performance across multiple programming languages and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-201: Our 7B model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the CoIR benchmark, setting a new standard for code retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-202: We furhter evaluate \modelname on RepoEval and SWE-Bench-Lite, demonstrating that improved retrieval significantly enhances end-to-end Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) performance for code-related tasks.
elements.sent-2411-203: Beyond the 7B model, we develop smaller models (400M, 2B) that surpass prior SOTA in code retrieval, while remaining competitive performance in text retrieval, highlighting their versatility across both domains.
elements.sent-2411-204: We transform general code-related tasks into a unified retrieval framework by representing each task as a structured retrieval pair \((Q, D)\), where \( Q \) is the query and \( D \) is the positive document.
elements.sent-2411-205: The retrieval process is formulated as a ranking problem, where the objective is to learn a function \( f(Q, D) \) that assigns higher similarity scores to relevant pairs than that of irrelevant ones.
elements.sent-2411-206: To train a unified retrieval model for both code and text, we transform all retrieval tasks into a query-document matching problem, where a query \( Q \) retrieves a relevant document \( D \) from a candidate set \( \mathcal{D \).}
elements.sent-2411-207: This formulation supports Text-to-Text, Text-to-Code, Code-to-Text, and Code-to-Code retrieval within a single framework.
elements.sent-2411-208: To better capture semantic relationships across entire input sequences, we incorporate bidirectional attention into a causal LLM~\cite{behnamghader2024llm2vec , enhancing its ability to encode contextual information for retrieval tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-209: Each query \( Q \) and document \( D \) are encoded using this pre-trained LLM, producing representations: \begin{equation}q = LLM_{\theta + \Delta\theta}(Q), \quad d = LLM_{\theta + \Delta\theta}(D)\end{equation} The similarity between query and document is measured using cosine similarity: \begin{equation}D^* = \arg\max_{D \in \mathcal{D}} \text{sim}(q, d), \quad \text{sim}(q, d) = \frac{q \cdot d}{\|q\| \|d\|}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}D^* = \arg\max_{D \in D} sim(q, d), \quad sim(q, d) = \frac{q \cdot d}{\|q\| \|d\|}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-210: To progressively enhance retrieval capabilities, we adopt a multi-stage training approach, utilizing distinct LoRA~\cite{hulora adapters at each stage.}
elements.sent-2411-211: In each stage, the previously trained LoRA weights are merged into the base model before initializing a new adapter, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:multistage .}
elements.sent-2411-212: This approach enables the model to iteratively adapt to different retrieval objectives without catastrophic forgetting~\cite{magistri2024empirical,han2024slim.}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.52\textwidth]{figure/multistage.pdf} \caption{The illustration depicts multi-stage training, showing the data used at each stage, the LoRA adapters applied, and the continuous optimizer with its learning rate progression.} \label{fig:multistage}
elements.subsubsection-2411-005: Stage 1: Supervised Contrastive Pretraining on Text Data
elements.sent-2411-213: We initialize a LoRA adapter \( \Delta\theta_1 \) and freeze the LLM $\theta$ and train $\Delta \theta_1$ on text-based retrieval tasks using a contrastive learning objective: \begin{equation} \mathcal{L} = - \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(q_i, d_i^+))} {\exp(\text{sim}(q_i, d_i^+)) + \sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp(\text{sim}(q_i, d_j^-))}\end{equation} where $d_i^+$ is a positive document, and $d_j^-$ represents negative documents, and $N$ denotes the batch size.
elements.sent-2411-214: The model learns to align text queries with their corresponding text-based documents, establishing a strong foundation for language-based retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-215: After training, we merge the trained adapter \( \Delta\theta_1 \) into the base LLM model: \begin{equation}\theta \leftarrow \theta + \Delta\theta_1\end{equation} and initialize a new LoRA adapter \( \Delta\theta_2 \) to learn retrieval for both code and text.
elements.sent-2411-216: This setup enables the model to mitigate text-only biases while preserving most of its learned representations.
elements.sent-2411-217: To ensure a smooth transition between training phases, we continue optimizing the second-stage LoRA adapter \( \Delta\theta_2 \) while retaining the gradient contributions from the first-stage adapter \( \Delta\theta_1 \), which remains fixed within the model.
elements.sent-2411-218: Specifically, we update \( \Delta\theta_2 \) while preserving the learning rate \( r^{(1) \) and the accumulated gradient from \( \Delta\theta_1 \): \begin{equation}\Delta\theta_2 \leftarrow \text{Update}(\Delta\theta_2, \nabla L(\theta_1, \theta_2), r^{(1)})\end{equation} where \( \nabla L(\theta_1, \theta_2) \) represents the gradient influenced by both adapters, but only \( \Delta\theta_2 \) is actively updated.}
elements.sent-2411-219: This approach stabilizes optimization, preventing abrupt parameter shifts while allowing a smooth adaptation to code retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-220: By preserving the previous learning rate and gradient continuity, we mitigate convergence instability and facilitate efficient adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-221: This multi-stage approach enables the model to iteratively improve retrieval performance while preventing overfitting to any single modality.
elements.sent-2411-222: By leveraging LoRA adapters at different stages and maintaining a smooth learning rate transition, we ensure efficient adaptation with minimal overhead, resulting in more robust text and code retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-223: Larger batch sizes improve contrastive learning by incorporating more diverse negatives~\cite{chen2022we , but GPU memory limits expansion.}
elements.sent-2411-224: To overcome this, we use GradCache~\cite{gao2021scaling , which reduces memory overhead, enabling larger effective batch sizes.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Let \( Q / D \) denote the full batch of queries and documents, partitioned into sub-batches (\( Q = \{\hat{Q _1, \hat{Q _2, \dots, \hat{Q _M\} \), \( D = \{\hat{D _1, \hat{D _2, \dots, \hat{D _M\} \)) to fit memory constraints.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-226: Training follows three steps: Graph-less Forward Pass Each sub-batch is encoded into embeddings \( q_i \) and \( d_i \) without computing encoder gradients, reducing memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-227: Gradient Computation and Caching For each query \( q_i \) in sub-batch \( \hat{Q _j \), we compute and cache gradients w.r.t.}
elements.sent-2411-228: GradCache enables larger in-batch negatives, improving contrastive learning efficiency while staying within GPU limits, essential for scaling text and code retrieval.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figure/code-embed.pdf} \caption{The code training data of \modelname contains four parts: Text-to-Code, Code-to-Code, Code-to-Text and Hybrid Code. Each Categories contains several types of code tasks.} \label{fig:frame}
elements.sent-2411-229: We organize retrieval tasks into distinct settings by converting various code-related tasks into a unified retrieval format.
elements.sent-2411-230: In this format, the input is treated as query (\( Q \)), and the expected output or relevant content is treated as document (\( D \)).
elements.sent-2411-231: This transformation allows the model to generalize across various retrieval tasks in both text and code domains.
elements.sent-2411-232: To improve retrieval across both code and text domains, we incorporate text-to-text retrieval tasks from the BEIR datasets~\cite{thakur2beir .}
elements.sent-2411-233: This ensures model robustness in general document retrieval while maintaining strong performance in code retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-234: We unify multiple code generation and classification tasks into the \((Q, D)\) format for code retrieval training, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:frame .}
elements.sent-2411-235: Text-to-Code Retrieval involves retrieving relevant code snippets given a natural language query.
elements.sent-2411-236: Tasks such as code contest generation~\cite{billah2024large,kadir2024exploring take problem descriptions as \( Q \) and map them to the correct implementation as \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-237: Similarly, in Text-to-SQL~\cite{finegan2018improving,li2024can , a user query in natural language serves as \( Q \), and the corresponding SQL query is treated as \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-238: Code-to-Text Retrieval retrieves human-readable descriptions for given code snippets.
elements.sent-2411-239: In code summarization~\cite{sontakke2022code,sun2024extractive , a code snippet serves as \( Q \), and the corresponding documentation or summary is \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-240: Code-to-Code Retrieval represents retrieving functionally equivalent or semantically related code snippets.
elements.sent-2411-241: In code translation~\cite{pan2024lost,karanjai2024solmover , the source code in one language is \( Q \), and its equivalent implementation in another language is \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-242: Code completion~\cite{ding2024crosscodeeval,phan2024repohyper,liurepobench uses the incomplete code fragment as \( Q \) and the correct completion as \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-243: Code clone detection~\cite{martinez2024source retrieves functionally similar code, treating the reference snippet as \( Q \) and the detected duplicate as \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-244: Hybrid Code Retrieval supports mixed queries containing both text and code.
elements.sent-2411-245: In code agent conversation~\cite{arteaga2024support,jin2024teach , user prompts containing explanations or code snippets act as \( Q \), while the retrieved response (either code or text) serves as \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-246: In code issue fixing~\cite{yang2024swe,jimenezswe , the hybrid query consists of an error message and buggy code as \( Q \), while the corrected version of the code is \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-247: By consolidating diverse retrieval tasks into a unified framework, we effectively streamline contrastive model training, thereby facilitating seamless adaptation across a wide range of programming scenarios while ensuring high retrieval accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-248: Evaluation Benchmarks We mainly use two benchmarks to evaluate code and text retrieval performance.
elements.sent-2411-249: It supports retrieval over a wide range of datasets and provides a standardized benchmark for evaluating text retrieval models across different domains.
elements.sent-2411-250: Implementation Details We conduct general training on our proposed code and text pair dataset using three model sizes: 400M, 2B, and 7B.
elements.sent-2411-251: The more implementation details can be found in Appendix~\ref{section:implementation .}
elements.sent-2411-252: Baseline Models For code-domain-specific models, we included UniXcoder~\citep{guo2022unixcoder , Voyage-Code-002\footnote{\url{https://blog.voyageai.com/2024/01/23/voyage-code-2-elevate-your-code-retrieval/ and CodeSage-large-v2~\citep{zhangcode , all pre-trained on code data, serving as strong baselines for comparison.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-264: To evaluate the effectiveness of our multi-stage training approach, we conduct an ablation study on four settings:.
elements.sent-2411-265: Single-stage (Baseline): Trained in a single phase using a mixture of text and code retrieval data with a single LoRA adapter.
elements.sent-2411-266: Two-stage (Same LoRA): The first stage is trained on text retrieval; the second stage continues with mixed text and code retrieval, reusing the same LoRA adapter across both stages.
elements.sent-2411-267: Two-stage (Different Optimizers): Uses different LoRA adapters for each stage, with separate optimizers for each.
elements.sent-2411-268: Proposed Two-stage (Separate LoRA): Applies different LoRA adapters for each stage, using a continuous optimizer throughout training.

elements.sent-2411-285: In the underexplored field of code retrieval, we present \modelname, a family of code embedding models ranging from 400M to 7B parameters.
elements.sent-2411-286: Our unified training pipeline integrates multiple programming languages and reformulates diverse code-related tasks within a common retrieval framework.
elements.sent-2411-287: By enhancing retrieval capabilities, we significantly improve end-to-end retrieval-augmented generation for code-related tasks.
elements.sent-2411-288: Bridging the gap between text and code retrieval, we release our models to foster research and innovation in developer tools and programming language understanding.. .
elements.sent-2411-289: Text-to-Code Retrieval: Text2SQL: We follow Text2SQL works in~\citet{yu2018spider and~\citet{zhong2017seq2sql , converting the text part into the queries to retrieval SQL as the documents, derived from Wikipedia data.}}
elements.sent-2411-290: In contrast, the CoIR benchmark uses synthetic-text2sql, generated using weak and strong LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-291: Code Contest: We extract the Code Contest tasks in the~\citet{khan2024xcodeeval , which has 11 programming language like java, c, c\# et al. While CoIR benchmark use APPS, which is the interview question only in python programming language.}
elements.sent-2411-292: Code-to-Text Retrieval: Code Summary: We follow~\citet{hu2018summarizing and~\citet{liuretrieval , which summarize code based on associated documentation, while CoIR utilizes a CodeSearchNet variant with code-comment pairs for the summary task.}}
elements.sent-2411-293: Code Clone Detection: We follow~\citet{svajlenko2015evaluating and \cite{lu1codexglue to convert the code as query and its clone code as the docs ; CoIR has no dataset for this task.}}
elements.sent-2411-294: Code-to-Code Retrieval: Code Translation: We follows~\citet{khan2024xcodeeval and \cite{lu1codexglue , converting code in one language into queries and corresponding code in another language into documents.}}
elements.sent-2411-295: CoIR has no dataset for this task.
elements.sent-2411-296: Code Completion: We use the code completion tasks from~\citet{khan2024xcodeeval and \cite{lu1codexglue , while CoIR uses a modified CodeSearchNet, splitting code snippets into query-document pairs.}}
elements.sent-2411-297: Hybrid Code Retrieval: Code Issue Fix: We follow~\citet{khan2024xcodeeval and \cite{lu1codexglue to involve the code issue fix task; CoIR has no dataset for this task.}}
elements.sent-2411-298: Code Agent Conversation: We process the conversation data from \cite{togetherredpajama , filtering answers based on favorite scores and excluding data used in CoIR.}
